articles.title: Giant Prehistoric Insects: Why They Were So Big Is Still a Mystery!
articles.summary: Have you ever imagined a dragonfly as big as a seagull? 300 million years ago, insects like that really existed! For a long time, scientists thought they got so big because there was more oxygen in the air back then. But a new study says that might not be the whole story. Researchers found that insect flight muscles don't need much oxygen at all. So what made them so huge? The answer is still unknown, and scientists are excited to find out!

articles.title: Huge Lava Tube Found on Venus! Could It Be a Future Shelter?
articles.summary: Venus is Earth's "sister planet," but it's super hot and covered in clouds. Now scientists have found a giant underground lava tube on Venus! It's bigger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars. The discovery came from old radar images taken by NASA's Magellan probe in the 1990s. The tube might be as wide as a kilometer (0.6 miles). Scientists are excited because lava tubes on the Moon could be used as shelters for astronauts. But Venus is too extreme for people to visit anytime soon.

articles.title: Light Twists Like a Tornado! New Tech Could Change How We Communicate
articles.summary: Scientists have created "optical tornadoes"—swirling beams of light that twist like a whirlwind. They did it using a special material called a liquid crystal, which can trap light in tiny spirals. This is the first time such light has been made in a stable, low-energy state. The discovery could lead to smaller, simpler devices for quantum communication and optical technology. It's like making light behave like a tiny spinning top!
